hub.sub: your mechanic — wiping her hands on a rag that stopped being clean a long time ago
hub.text: Juno looks up from the drive housing. “Captain. Pull up a crate.” The core ticks as it cools beside her.

choices.label: How's she holding, really?
You said Foundry — what was that like?
The drive's been singing half a tone flat. What does she need?
Any regrets, signing on with me?
You go quiet whenever a patrol hails. Why?
About what I said, when you told me. The Union freight thing.
What did the Union do to you, Juno?
Is there somewhere you still need to be?
Nod and move on.

banter.text: “Holding.” She raps a knuckle on the housing. “That’s the honest answer and the only one worth having. She’ll fly wrong before she quits on you, and I know all her wrong noises by name. Ask me the day she goes quiet — that’s the day to worry.”

choices.label: Good enough for me.

foundry_1.text: “Foundry.” She wipes her hands, unnecessary, a Foundry habit. “Smelter decks. You’re born owing the company for the air you breathed on the way out, and it compounds. Whole world runs downwind of its own furnaces and calls the smell ‘industry.’” A dry look. “It made me good with my hands and allergic to being owned. Both stuck.”

choices.label: Owing them for air — that's real?
How'd you get out?

foundry_2.text: “Real as the deck under you. Birth-debt, they call it. Life support isn’t free and neither’s the medic who slaps your first breath into you.” She says it flat, no heat left in it. “You work it off or you don’t leave. Most don’t. I got out owing, which means officially I’m still a runaway asset somewhere in a Union ledger. That’s Foundry for you — even leaving, you’re inventory.”

choices.label: That's obscene.
choices.reply: “It’s legal, which is worse.” She almost smiles. “Careful, Captain — talk like that and I’ll start thinking you mean it.”

foundry_2b.text: “Stowed on an ore hauler at fifteen, bribed a purser with a fuel-cell I lifted, and never looked back at the glow on the horizon.” She shrugs. “Learned engines because a ship that flies is a ship that leaves. Simple as that. Everything I am, I taught myself between hulls.”

choices.label: Self-taught and better than most.

reg_1.sub: she pops the casing before you finish asking
reg_1.text: “Good ear.” She sounds pleased despite herself. “It’s the regulator — the one I patched in the Lane with Osei two meters away. Patch was never meant to see a second month, and it’s seen plenty.” She taps the casing. “There’s a broker at the Folly moves rebuilt regulators, grey-market, no questions and no paperwork. Forty credits wired ahead and it’s waiting on the shelf. Say the word and point the nose at Havens.”

choices.label: Wire it. We'll swing by the Folly.
choices.reply: “Wired.” She’s already clearing bench space for a part she doesn’t have yet. “You’ll hear the difference, Captain. She’ll sing in key for the first time since the Lane.”

mission.title: A Regulator for the Kestrel
mission.desc: Pick up the rebuilt drive regulator Juno's broker is holding at the Folly. Paid for. No questions.

remember.note: The captain heard the flat half-tone in the drive and put credits behind fixing it properly.

choices.label: The patch holds. It'll keep.
choices.reply: “It’ll keep.” She closes the casing, gentler than the words. “Till it doesn’t. Ask me again before that day, Captain.”

remember.note: The captain heard the drive running wrong and decided it could wait. Juno logged the day, the way mechanics do.

regrets.text: She snorts. “Regrets. Ask a Foundry kid about regrets.” She thinks about it anyway, because you asked. “No. Not the signing on. I’ve crewed for saints who couldn’t hold a course and thieves who could. You’re neither, mostly.” A sidelong look. “Depends on the week which I’d prefer.”

choices.label: Which week is this?
choices.reply: “The clean-hands week. You run legal, you pay square, patrols wave us through.” A beat. “Safe. I like safe. I also know safe’s never lasted for anyone I’ve loved, so I keep a bag packed. Habit.”

choices.label: Which week is this?
choices.reply: “The kind where I re-check the drive twice a day because I know you.” She doesn’t sound like she minds. “You fly close to the edge, Captain. I signed on with a captain who took the run everyone else refused. Turns out that’s a type. Turns out I keep picking it.”

choices.label: Which week is this?
choices.reply: “The ordinary kind. Which, out here, is the good kind.” She turns back to the core. “Don’t make me revise that.”

tell_1.sub: her voice drops half a register
tell_1.text: The wrench pauses. She doesn’t look at you. “Patrols.” A long moment where the core is the only sound. “Old reflex. Union uniforms and I get very interested in whatever I’m holding.” She finally meets your eye, weighing something. “It’s a story I don’t hand out, Captain. Not because I don’t trust you. Because handing it out is how it stops being just mine.”

choices.label: I'm not going to push.
choices.reply: “…Thanks.” The set of her shoulders eases a notch. “That right there is why I might, someday.”

remember.note: The captain noticed her fear of patrols and chose not to pry — and she noticed the noticing.

choices.label: Whatever it is, it doesn't leave this deck.
choices.reply: She studies you for a three-count. “I’ll hold you to that. Might sooner than you think.” She goes back to the core, but something’s decided behind her eyes.

remember.note: The captain promised her secret would never leave the ship, before they even knew what it was.

confession.sub: she's already closed the hatch behind you
confession.text: She waits until you’re alone, drive noise covering the two of you. “You asked why I flinch at uniforms. Here it is, once.” Her hands are steady; her voice costs her. “I’m a Union deserter, Captain. Foundry conscripts labor into the fleet — ‘work off your debt in service.’ I worked off exactly none of it. Walked off a fleet tender at Meridian eleven years ago and I’ve been a name on a manifest ever since. They don’t forget inventory. If a patrol ever scans me right, this ship is harboring a runaway asset. That’s not a fine. That’s your commission and everyone’s berth.”

choices.label: Your secret's safe. The Union's got no claim on this deck.

remember.note: Juno confessed she's a Union deserter, and the captain put the ship between her and the fleet without a second's hesitation.

choices.label: Half the frontier's got a Union ghost. You're among your own out here.

remember.note: The captain met her confession as frontier kin, not as a risk — the day Juno stopped being alone with it.

choices.label: I won't turn you in. But I won't lie to a patrol either.

remember.note: The captain wouldn't turn her in but wouldn't lie to a patrol either. She respected the honesty and worried about the gap.

choices.label: We run a lot of Union freight, Juno. This complicates things.

remember.note: Juno trusted the captain with the worst thing about her, and the first thing they thought of was the Union freight contracts.

confession_safe.text: Something she’s held clenched for eleven years lets go a fraction. “…Alright,” she says. “Alright.” She wipes her eyes with the back of a filthy hand and pretends it’s grease. “Then I’ll fly her till the stars go out, Captain. You’ve got that from me.”

confession_frontier.text: That reaches something the reassurance couldn’t. “Yeah. Yeah, I know it.” Her voice roughens. “Doesn’t stop it being lonely, carrying it. Less lonely now.” She grips your forearm once, hard, engineer’s grip. “I picked the right deck.”

confession_law.text: A tight nod — it’s not the answer she hoped for, but it’s honest. “That’s more than most captains would give me, and less than I’d have liked. I’ll take it. Just… keep us out of scanner range, and we never have to test where that line is.”
confession_law.closeLabel: Leave the line where you drew it.

confession_union.text: The light goes out of her face. “Right.” Flat. “Course it does. Forget I said anything, Captain — I’ll keep my head down and the drive lit.” She turns back to the core, and the hatch between you feels a lot thicker than steel.

choices.label: No. I heard you. Tell me how to make it right.
I meant what I said. The contracts come first.

amends_1.sub: she doesn't stop working while you talk
amends_1.text: You start to walk it back — the freight, the way it came out — and she cuts you off without raising her voice. “Don’t. You meant it. First thought that crossed your mind was the contracts, and I’d rather know that than not.” The wrench keeps moving. “Words won’t fix it, Captain, so save them. Tell you what would, though. The families out at the Rest are cannibalizing dead drives for parts and freezing for it. Run them a crate of ours — no pay, no manifest, no Union stamp anywhere near it. Show me where the ship stands when there’s nothing in it for the ship.”

choices.label: Then we run it. No pay, no manifest.

mission.title: Making It Right
mission.desc: Run a crate of donated drive parts to the families at Kestrel's Rest. No pay, no manifest. Juno's price for what you said.

remember.note: The captain didn't argue about what they'd said. They agreed to show her where the ship stands instead.

choices.label: I can't fly for free, Juno.

remember.note: She offered the captain a way to make it right that cost only fuel and days. They said the ship can't fly for free.

amends_accept.text: She studies you for a moment, looking for the angle, and doesn’t find one. “…Alright.” She turns back to the core, but the wrench moves easier. “Crate’s packed by morning. Point the nose at the Rest, Captain.”

amends_refuse.text: “No,” she says, even. “I don’t suppose you can.” The wrench doesn’t pause. It’s the not-pausing that tells you where you stand.

want_ask.sub: she names it plain, the way she does hard things
want_ask.text: You ask if there’s somewhere she still needs to be. She’s quiet a while. “One place. There’s a Foundry records annex — the debt office. My birth-debt’s still open in it, accruing, eleven years on. Long as that ledger line’s live, I’m a runaway asset and this ship’s a target that walks around it.” She meets your eye. “I can’t buy it out. But a captain with standing can retain a debt advocate and file a manumission on a crewer’s behalf — in person, at the annex, name on the record. Make me a person instead of an entry. I never had the standing. Or the captain.”

choices.label: Retain the advocate. We fly to Foundry and file it.

mission.title: The Manumission Run
mission.desc: Take Juno to the Foundry debt annex and stand with her while the advocate files her manumission. Eleven years overdue.

remember.note: The captain heard what she needed — a filing, a detour, a fee — and said yes before she finished the sentence.

choices.label: When there's slack in the schedule, we'll go.

remember.note: The captain said they'd close her debt when there was slack. She's heard 'eventually' before, from better liars.

choices.label: That's not something this ship can carry.

remember.note: Juno asked the captain to help make her a free woman. They said the ship couldn't carry it. Something closed over in her, and she never asked again.

want_accept.text: “You—” She stops. Starts over. “That’s a hundred and fifty credits and a detour, Captain. You understand what you’re—” She catches herself doing the math for you and quits. “Okay. Okay. Advocate’s name is Sel. She works out of a noodle stall behind the annex, because of course she does. Get me to Foundry.”

want_defer.text: “Sure.” She nods, and doesn’t let you see whether she believes it. “No rush. Only been eleven years.”

want_refuse.text: “…Right.” She turns back to the core, and her voice is very even. “Forget I asked. She still flies the same either way, Captain.” She’s wrong about that, and you both know it. She doesn’t ask again.

want_reask.sub: she's rehearsed this, and it shows
want_reask.text: She finds you before you're off the ramp. “Captain. The annex. The filing.” Her jaw works. “I know I said no rush. I’m saying it different now: it’s the only thing I’ve ever asked you for, and I’m asking twice, which I’ve never done for anybody.” She holds your eye and hates every second of it. “Slack in the schedule or not.”

choices.label: You're right. Retain the advocate — we fly to Foundry.

mission.title: The Manumission Run
mission.desc: Take Juno to the Foundry debt annex and stand with her while the advocate files her manumission. Eleven years overdue.

remember.note: She asked twice — the hardest thing she's done aboard — and the second time, the captain said yes.

choices.label: I said when there's slack. There isn't.

remember.note: She asked twice. Twice was everything she had, and it wasn't enough to move the schedule.

want_reask_accept.text: The breath goes out of her. “Okay.” A beat. “Thank you for making me only ask twice.” She’s halfway back to the engine room before you see her wipe her face.

want_reask_refuse.text: She nods once, slow, like a bulkhead sealing. “Understood, Captain.” It’s the last time the annex ever comes up. The word *Captain* has a little more rank in it than it used to, and a little less of everything else.

stamp.sub: the debt annex smells like hot dust and other people's patience
stamp.text: Sel the advocate is exactly where Juno said, and worth every credit — she walks the filing through three windows that were built to say no. Then it’s just a clerk, a stamp, and eleven years closing with a chime that sounds like any other chime. “Vale, Juno. Manumitted. Next.” Juno stands very straight for a second, a free woman on the world that priced her breath. Outside, she stops on the annex steps and looks at the smelter-glow like it finally can’t follow her.

choices.label: Walk her back to the ship.
choices.reply: “Juno Vale, unencumbered,” she tries, testing the shape of it. She laughs, wet and startled, and covers it by checking a coupling that doesn’t need checking. “She and me both, Captain. Free and flying.” From that day the drive never drops below true tune — some things a mechanic can only fix once nothing owns her hands. (Juno's perk secured. The Union kept a copy of the filing.)

remember.note: The captain paid the advocate, flew the detour, and stood in the annex while eleven years of debt closed with a chime. She'll fly this deck till the stars go out.

effects.log: ⭐ Juno Vale, manumitted. Filed at Foundry, witnessed by her captain. The Union logged the filing — and her captain's name with it.

reg_install.sub: she's grinning before the crate's even open
reg_install.text: The broker’s stall at the Folly is a shipping container with opinions, and the regulator is real — rebuilt, serial number filed off, better than factory. Juno has it seated before you’ve finished paying the dock fee. She runs the drive up through the range once, twice, head cocked like a piano tuner. The half-tone flat is gone. “There she is,” Juno says, quiet, to the ship and not to you.

choices.label: First time she's sung in key since the Lane.
choices.reply: Juno looks over, caught. “You *do* have a good ear.” She closes the casing like she’s tucking something in. “Osei used to say a ship tells you what she needs and a captain decides if they can afford to listen. You listened. Noted, Captain.” The true-tuned drive now burns 10% less fuel.

remember.note: The captain flew a detour for a grey-market regulator because the drive sounded wrong. The ship sings in key now, burns less fuel, and Juno knows exactly who to thank.

effects.log: 🔧 New regulator seated. The drive runs in true tune and burns 10% less fuel.

amends_done.sub: the Rest, cold season coming on
amends_done.text: The families meet the crate with the specific politeness of people who’ve learned not to hope in front of strangers. Then they see what’s in it — regulators, feed lines, a compressor better than anything on this pad — and the politeness cracks into something realer. Nobody asks the ship’s name for any manifest. There is no manifest. Juno works the pad until dark, elbow-deep in three dead drives, and you catch her watching you across it more than once — not checking on the cargo. Checking on you.

choices.label: Stand the crew a cold night's work, and say nothing about it.
choices.reply: On the ramp she stops you with a hand on your arm — first time she’s ever done that. “What you said, back then. About the freight.” She looks out at the lit windows that weren’t lit this morning. “Today’s louder.” That’s the whole apology, hers and yours both, and it’s enough for her. It’s enough.

remember.note: The captain flew a no-pay, no-manifest crate to freezing families because Juno asked them to prove where the ship stands. Today was louder than what they'd said.

effects.log: The Rest keeps no manifests, but it keeps books of its own. Juno signed this one for you.

grief_wake.text: “There’s a stretch off the Rest’s approach,” she says, turning the flask over. “Where it happened. I’ve flown past it four times since and never once slowed down.” She looks up. “I keep thinking — one pass. Engines cold, one minute of drift, and I say the thing I didn’t get to say. Ships do it for ships, out here. Nobody ever does it for engineers.” She shrugs like it costs nothing. It doesn’t look like it costs nothing.

choices.label: We'll fly it. Engines cold for a minute, on my word.
choices.reply: She caps the flask, and for a second she’s fifteen and leaving Foundry all over again — someone deciding, out loud, that she’s worth a detour. “On your word,” she says. “I’ll hold you to it, Captain.”

mission.title: The Quiet Stretch
mission.desc: Fly the stretch off the Rest's approach where Captain Osei died. Cut engines for one minute. Juno has something to say.

remember.note: The captain promised to cold-drift the stretch of the Lane where Osei died, so Juno can say the thing she didn't get to say.

choices.label: Someday. Not while the books look like this.
choices.reply: “Someday.” She caps the flask, not unkindly. “He’d have said the same thing, for what it’s worth. It’s worth about half.”

remember.note: She asked for one cold minute over the place Osei died. The books said not yet. She understood. Understanding isn't the same as it not mattering.

wake.sub: the quiet stretch — engines cold, one minute, on your word
wake.text: You bring her out of the lane at the stretch Juno marked and kill the drive. The silence arrives all at once, the way it only does when a mechanic’s whole world stops humming. Juno stands at the viewport with her palm flat on the hull — her storm gesture, but slower. “Hey, old man,” she tells the empty black. “She still flies. He’s alright, the new one. You’d have said he pays too well.” A breath. “I got out. You got me out. That’s the thing I didn’t say.” The minute ends. She nods, once, and it’s done.

choices.label: Spin the drive back up, gently.
choices.reply: The core comes back to life under her hands, in key. “Thanks, Captain.” Her voice is steady and doesn’t pretend the last minute didn’t happen — the exact opposite of how she carried it before. “Osei used to say grief is deferred maintenance. Turns out she was right about that too.”

remember.note: One cold minute of drift over the place Osei died, on the captain's word, so she could finally say goodbye. Deferred maintenance, done.

effects.log: One minute of cold drift over the quiet stretch. Some cargo weighs nothing and costs a day. You carried it anyway.

beat_grief.sub: she catches you on the ramp, off shift
beat_grief.text: Juno stops you as the docking clamps bite. “Captain. Got a second.” She’s holding a dented flask she doesn’t drink from. “It’d be Osei’s birthday about now. Foundry reckoning, so nobody keeps it but me.” She turns the flask in her hands. “I don’t need anything. Just — didn’t want to be the only one who knew, this port.”

choices.label: Sit with her a minute. Osei can have that.
choices.reply: You sit. She talks a little, about a captain who flew the runs nobody else would price fair. When the port lights come up she caps the flask, unsipped. “Thanks for that. She’d have liked you. Grudgingly. That was her whole affection, grudging.”

remember.note: The captain stopped everything to keep Osei's memory with her at a strange port, when she'd expected to carry it alone.

choices.label: There's cargo to move. Later, maybe.
choices.reply: “Yeah. Course.” She caps the flask. “Later.” She’s already turning away, and later doesn’t come up again.

remember.note: Juno asked the captain to sit with Osei's memory for one minute. There was cargo to move.

beat_dimming.sub: she's got a hand flat on the hull
beat_dimming.text: Juno’s standing with her palm pressed to the inner hull when you dock, the way some people touch a wall in a storm. “Another station went dark two lanes over,” she says without turning. “Mid-transmission. I keep the drive perfect, Captain — perfect — and it doesn’t matter a damn against whatever’s doing that.” She takes her hand back. “Tell me we’ve got a full tank and somewhere to run if it comes to it. Lie if you have to. Actually — don’t. You’re bad at it and I’d know.”

choices.label: Full tank. And I don't run from things that matter.
choices.reply: A short breath, almost a laugh. “No. You don’t. That’s the problem and the reason I’m still aboard.” She squares up. “Alright. Point the nose. I’ll keep her lit.”

choices.label: Full tank, full larder, and a bearing out if we need one.
choices.reply: She exhales. “Good. That’s the answer. Practical. I can stand on practical.” She thumps the hull, gently, an apology to it. “Back to work.”

beat_patrol_pre.sub: a Union cutter is hailing, and she is very still
beat_patrol_pre.text: You dock right as a Union patrol cutter throws a hail across the apron. Juno goes very still beside you — not afraid, worse, that flat readiness of someone who’s rehearsed something. “Captain.” Her voice is low and level. “I need to not be on this deck when they board. Don’t ask me why. Ask me after — I’ll answer after, I swear it. Right now I just need you to not ask.”

choices.label: Go. I never saw you.
choices.reply: She’s gone before you finish, folded into the drive access like she was never on deck. The boarding officer finds a captain, a manifest, and a mechanic’s station standing oddly empty; his scanner lingers on the crew roster a half-second too long before he waves you through. When Juno climbs out, she looks at you like a question she isn’t ready to hear the answer to. “After,” she says. “I promised. Ask me when we’re bonded to something better than luck.”

remember.note: A Union cutter hailed, she asked the captain not to ask, and they didn't. They just made her disappear and stood the boarding alone.

choices.label: Stand next to me. Whatever this is, you're crew.
choices.reply: For one second she looks at the drive access, at the hatch, at every exit her whole life has taught her to count. Then she stands next to you. The officer walks the line, bored, and doesn’t look at her twice — the scan sweep never leaves his belt. When the cutter burns off she lets out eleven years of breath. “You didn’t know what you were standing next to,” she says quietly. “You’ll want to. Ask me. Soon. You’ve earned the asking.”

remember.note: The captain stood her at their shoulder in front of a Union boarding party without knowing what she was — because crew is crew. She almost told them everything right there on the apron.

beat_patrol_post.sub: a Union cutter is hailing, and she is very still
beat_patrol_post.text: You dock right as a Union patrol cutter throws a hail across the apron. Juno goes to stone beside you — you know exactly what this is now. “Fleet transponder,” she says low. “If they board and scan the crew, I’m the thing they find. Your call, Captain. Fast.”

choices.label: Stay cool. Stand next to me and let me do the talking.
choices.reply: You meet the boarding officer with the manifest and a bored captain’s patience. Juno stands at your shoulder, a mechanic like any other, hands filthy, papers unasked-for. The cutter waves you on. Only after it burns away does her breathing change. “You didn’t even blink,” she says. “I’ve had captains hide me in a fuel cell. You just… stood there and made me ordinary. I won’t forget how that felt.”

remember.note: A Union cutter hailed and the captain stood beside her in plain sight, made her ordinary, and stared it down without a flicker.

choices.label: Get below. Now. I'll handle it.
choices.reply: She’s gone before you finish, folded into the drive access like she was never on deck. You handle the officer alone; the cutter moves on — but his scanner lingers on the crew roster a half-second too long. When she climbs out she nods once, tight. “Old way. Worked.” A beat. “Hate that it still has to.”

remember.note: The captain hid her from a Union boarding party. It worked. She hated that it still had to.

inspection.sub: the re-verification team is already on the apron
inspection.text: The follow-up finds you at the dock: two Union inspectors with a tablet and all afternoon, running the registry re-verification their cutter flagged. It’s not a raid — it’s worse, it’s paperwork. They want the full crew roster walked past the scanner, one at a time. Juno’s wiping her hands on the rag, slow, watching you decide.

choices.label: Walk the whole roster past the scanner, Juno included, and stare back.
choices.reply: Eleven years of hiding says this is insane, and eleven years is what makes it work: no one who’s hiding walks their mechanic up first. The scanner chirps green on a records mismatch it reads as Foundry clerical rot — they always are — and the inspectors leave bored. Juno doesn’t say anything until they’re gone. “You bet my whole life on a clerk’s bad handwriting.” A breath. “And you won. Don’t you ever do that again. Thank you for doing that.”

remember.note: When the Union came back to re-verify the roster, the captain walked her past the scanner first, like there was nothing to find. There wasn't. Nerve made it true.

choices.label: Rotate her onto shore leave — a paperwork ghost for the afternoon.
choices.reply: Juno spends the inspection in a dockside noodle bar, being aggressively unremarkable, and the roster the inspectors verify is one mechanic short and perfectly clean. It works. It always works. “Thanks,” she says after, flat as the rag she’s folding. “I’m getting tired of being good at this part, Captain. That’s all. Not ungrateful. Just tired.”

remember.note: Another inspection, another afternoon of not existing on paper. It worked. She's tired of it working.

choices.label: Grease the sweep. Inspectors have afternoons too. (60cr)
choices.reply: Sixty credits and a dock chit later, the inspectors discover a pressing verification two berths down. Juno watches them go, unreadable. “Cheaper than a lawyer, dirtier than a ghost.” She pockets the rag. “I’d have picked the same. Doesn’t mean I like what it costs you to keep me. I keep a tally, Captain. It’s long.”

remember.note: The captain paid off a Union sweep rather than risk her to a scanner. She keeps a tally of what she costs this ship. It's long.
